 {"id":"bd-1oyeg","title":"Parser: DA/DSR device queries + character set designation (G0/G1) + single shifts","description":"## Background
The VT parser handles 56 action variants covering most core terminal sequences, but lacks several important features needed for application compatibility.

## Gaps to Fill

### Device Attribute / Status Reports (Tier 1 — critical handshake)
- DA1 (CSI c / CSI 0 c): Primary device attributes — many CLI programs send this as a startup handshake
- DA2 (CSI > c / CSI > 0 c): Secondary device attributes (terminal type/version)
- DSR (CSI 5 n): Device status report — "are you OK?" query
- CPR (CSI 6 n): Cursor position report request

### Character Set Designation (Tier 1 — affects rendering)
- G0 designate (ESC ( B / ESC ( 0): ASCII / DEC Special Graphics
- G1 designate (ESC ) B / ESC ) 0): ASCII / DEC Special Graphics
- These are used by ncurses, vim, tmux for box-drawing characters

### Single Shift (Tier 2 — completeness)
- SS2 (ESC N): Single shift to G2
- SS3 (ESC O): Single shift to G3

## Deliverables
- New Action variants for DA1, DA2, DSR, CPR, DesignateCharset, SingleShift
- Parser dispatch for all new sequences
- Unit tests for each new sequence
- Conformance fixtures for key sequences
- Wire new actions in test harnesses (no-op in harness is fine)

## Testing
- cargo fmt --check
- cargo check --all-targets
- cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings
- cargo test -p frankenterm-core","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"FuchsiaCreek","created_at":"2026-02-09T04:09:19.791783954Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T04:17:52.264271402Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T04:17:47.890125157Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":1048,"issue_id":"bd-1oyeg","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Completed: 8 new Action variants (DeviceAttributes, DeviceAttributesSecondary, DeviceStatusReport, CursorPositionReport, DesignateCharset, SingleShift2, SingleShift3), CSI dispatch for c/>c/n, ESC dispatch for (/)/*//+/N/O, EscCharset state, reply engine integration, 15 unit tests, 3 conformance fixtures.  {"id":"bd-1oz","title":"Implement LRU width cache for text measurement","description":"# Implement LRU Width Cache for Text Measurement

## Background
Plan Section 1.4 and Appendix D.3 explicitly mention the "LRU width cache" from rich_rust as a key optimization. Text width measurement is a hot path (called for every render) and must be cached.

## Why LRU Cache?
- Unicode width calculation is expensive (grapheme segmentation + width lookup)
- Same text strings appear repeatedly (labels, status text, etc.)
- Cache hit rate expected to be high for typical UIs
- Prevents O(n) per-frame width recalculation

## Design
```rust
/// Width cache with LRU eviction
pub struct WidthCache {
    cache: LinkedHashMap<u64, usize>,  // hash -> width
    capacity: usize,
}

impl WidthCache {
    pub fn new(capacity: usize) -> Self {
        Self {
            cache: LinkedHashMap::with_capacity(capacity),
            capacity,
        }
    }

    /// Get cached width or compute and cache
    pub fn get_or_compute(&mut self, text: &str, compute: impl FnOnce() -> usize) -> usize {
        let hash = hash_text(text);
        
        if let Some(&width) = self.cache.get(&hash) {
            // Move to front (LRU refresh)
            self.cache.get_refresh(&hash);
            return width;
        }
        
        let width = compute();
        
        // Evict if over capacity
        while self.cache.len() >= self.capacity {
            self.cache.pop_front();
        }
        
        self.cache.insert(hash, width);
        width
    }
    
    pub fn clear(&mut self) {
        self.cache.clear();
    }
}

// Use FNV or xxhash for fast hashing
fn hash_text(text: &str) -> u64 {
    use std::hash::{Hash, Hasher};
    let mut hasher = FnvHasher::default();
    text.hash(&mut hasher);
    hasher.finish()
}
```

## Integration Points
- `RenderContext` holds a `RefCell<WidthCache>`
- `Text::cell_length()` uses the cache
- `Segment::cell_length()` uses the cache
- Cache is cleared on theme change (fonts can affect width)

## Cache Sizing
- Default: 1024 entries (covers typical UI with headroom)
- Configurable via `WidthCache::new(capacity)`
- Memory footprint: ~32 bytes per entry (hash + width + list pointers)

## Performance Requirements
- Cache hit: < 50ns (hash + lookup)
- Cache miss: < 5µs (grapheme segmentation + width calculation)
- Hash collision handling: rare, just recompute

## Tests Required
- [ ] Cache hits return correct cached value
- [ ] Cache misses compute and cache
- [ ] LRU eviction works correctly
- [ ] Clear() empties cache
- [ ] Thread safety if needed (RefCell for single-threaded)
- [ ] Performance benchmarks

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] LRU cache implemented with configurable capacity
- [ ] Integrated into text measurement hot path
- [ ] Performance benchmarks show significant speedup
- [ ] No correctness regressions in width measurement

 {"id":"bd-1plj","title":"Async Task Manager E2E: Anytime-Valid Flake Detector (e-process)","description":"## Goal
Detect flaky timing regressions in E2E tests without inflating false positives, using anytime-valid statistics.

## Core Idea (Alien Artifact)
Compute an e-value for latency/outcome deviations during E2E runs. For sub-Gaussian residuals r_t (mean 0 under null), use:
- e_t = exp(lambda * r_t - (lambda^2 * sigma^2) / 2)
- E_t = product_{i=1..t} e_i
Fail early only when E_t > 1/alpha, preserving validity under optional stopping.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Early fail only with strong evidence; no false alarms in stable runs.
- Works with variable-length test runs.
- Logs e-value trajectory and decision point.

## Tests (REQUIRED)
- unit_eprocess_threshold: failure at E_t > 1/alpha.
- unit_eprocess_nonnegative: e-values never negative.
- unit_optional_stopping: stopping early preserves decision validity (simulated).

## E2E (REQUIRED)
- Inject synthetic latency spikes; verify early failure and log evidence.
- No spike: no failure; log shows stable e-values.

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures.
- Property tests: invariants (idempotence, monotonicity, determinism) where applicable.
- Fuzz tests: parsers/state machines and any input-facing logic.
- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.

 {"id":"bd-1qb","title":"Implement Image widget with protocol detection","description":"PHASE 7 - EXTRAS: IMAGE WIDGET

## Background
Image widget displays images in terminal using Kitty Graphics Protocol, iTerm2 inline images, or Sixel fallback. Requires protocol detection and graceful degradation.

## Technical Requirements
- Protocol detection:
  - Kitty Graphics Protocol (XTGETTCAP query)
  - iTerm2 inline images (TERM_PROGRAM check)
  - Sixel (DA2 response parsing)
  - Fallback: ASCII/Braille representation
- Image loading via image crate
- Resize/fit modes: Contain, Cover, Stretch, None
- Aspect ratio preservation
- Partial update for animated GIFs
- Memory management for large images

## Design Rationale
Image support is a premium feature gated behind 'image' flag. Protocol detection happens once at startup and caches result. Fallback ensures graceful degradation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Protocol detection and caching
- [ ] Kitty Graphics Protocol implementation
- [ ] iTerm2 inline image implementation
- [ ] Sixel fallback (optional sub-feature)
- [ ] ASCII fallback when no protocol available
- [ ] Resize mode configuration
- [ ] Aspect ratio handling
- [ ] Feature gate: image

## Performance Budget
- Protocol detection: < 50ms (one-time)
- Image encode/transmit: proportional to size

 {"id":"bd-1vb18","title":"Fix emoji-triggered border corruption in demo showcase (keep emojis)","description":"Problem
- Demo showcase has border misalignment/corruption in File Browser and Markdown screens when emojis are present. Emojis must remain.

Likely Causes
1) Several screens try to "clear" the full area using Paragraph::new("") + bg style, but Paragraph only paints style and does not clear cell content. With Flex gaps between panels, stale border characters from prior frames/screens can bleed into those gaps and appear as misaligned borders.
2) Buffer::print_text_clipped (ftui-render) advances by grapheme_width() but may only write the first char for multi-codepoint graphemes (e.g. VS16 sequences like "⚙️"), leaving holes that can preserve stale border chars.

Fix
- Clear demo screen root areas with actual space cells (bg=surface) so gaps are deterministically blank.
- Make Buffer::print_text_clipped fill any extra width when falling back to first char so no holes remain (and add a regression test).

Acceptance
- File Browser and Markdown screens render without stray/misaligned borders in gaps.
- Emojis remain enabled in file browser icons and unicode showcase.
- cargo fmt/check/clippy/test pass.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"GrayFalcon","created_at":"2026-02-06T02:01:09.704771751Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T02:34:21.706728130Z","closed_at":"2026-02-06T02:34:21.706701631Z","close_reason":"Completed (emoji border corruption fixed: VS16 width policy + buffer filler + empty paragraph clears)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["borders","demo-showcase","emoji","render","text"]}

 {"id":"bd-1xo","title":"Windows Terminal compatibility layer","description":"PHASE 7 - POLISH: WINDOWS SUPPORT

## Background
Windows Terminal v1 support is deferred but must have clear integration path. Modern Windows Terminal supports ANSI, older conhost needs different handling.

## Technical Requirements (v1 Scope - Windows Terminal Only)
- Detect Windows Terminal vs legacy conhost
- Enable VT processing mode via SetConsoleMode
- Handle Windows-specific escape sequences
- PTY spawning via ConPTY API
- Signal handling differences (Ctrl+C, Ctrl+Break)
- Path handling for Windows conventions

## Out of Scope for v1
- Legacy conhost support (Windows 7/8)
- PowerShell-specific handling
- Windows-specific widgets

## Design Rationale
Per ADR-004, Windows v1 targets modern Windows Terminal only. This dramatically simplifies implementation while covering majority of Windows developer use.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Windows Terminal detection
- [ ] VT mode enabling
- [ ] ConPTY integration for PTY tests
- [ ] Signal handling shim
- [ ] CI testing on Windows
- [ ] Feature gate: windows

## Testing
- GitHub Actions Windows runner
- Manual testing on Windows Terminal
- Document known limitations

 {"id":"bd-2kj","title":"Build Agent Harness reference application (ftui-harness)","description":"# Build Agent Harness Reference Application (ftui-harness)

## Background
Plan Section 12.4 and Chapter 17 define the Agent Harness as the PRIMARY design forcing function. This is not "just an example" - it is the proof that ftui works for its primary use case: Claude Code / Codex-style agent UIs.

## Purpose
The agent harness reference application demonstrates:
1. Inline mode with streaming logs and stable UI chrome
2. No flicker, no cursor corruption, reliable cleanup
3. Real-world patterns for agent harness builders

## Minimum Harness Primitives (from Plan 0.8.3)
- `write_log(text)` - scrollback-native (inline mode)
- `present_ui(frame)` - atomic and flicker-resistant
- Optional "modal alt-screen" for complex UI interactions
- Child-process capture via PTY (feature gated)

## Application Structure

```
ftui-harness/
  src/
    main.rs           # Entry point
    app.rs            # Application model
    components/
      log_viewer.rs   # Scrolling log display
      status_bar.rs   # Top status line
      tool_status.rs  # Tool execution indicator
      input_line.rs   # User input/prompt
    commands.rs       # Command handling
    pty_capture.rs    # Feature-gated PTY support
  examples/
    minimal.rs        # Bare minimum harness
    streaming.rs      # High-volume log streaming
    modal.rs          # Modal alt-screen demo
```

## UI Layout

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ [Status] Model: claude-3  │  Tool: Running grep...  │  Memory: 2.1GB │ 23:45│
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Searching for patterns in codebase...                                     │
│  Found 42 matches in src/                                                  │
│  Analyzing results...                                                      │
│  [more log output continues...]                                            │
│                                                                             │
│                                                                             │
│                                                                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ > Enter command:                                                        [⠋]│
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Regions
- **Status Bar (1 line)**: Model info, tool status, memory, time
- **Log Region (variable)**: Scrolling log viewer, grows with terminal
- **Input Region (1-2 lines)**: User prompt + spinner

## Application Model

```rust
use ftui::{App, Cmd, Event, Frame, Model, ScreenMode, Result};

pub struct AgentHarness {
    // UI Components
    log_viewer: LogViewer,
    status_bar: StatusBar,
    input_line: InputLine,
    spinner: Spinner,
    
    // State
    model_name: String,
    current_tool: Option<String>,
    memory_usage: u64,
    
    // Mode
    screen_mode: ScreenMode,
    ui_height: u16,
}

impl Model for AgentHarness {
    type Message = HarnessMessage;
    
    fn init(&mut self) -> Cmd<Self::Message> {
        Cmd::batch(vec![
            Cmd::tick(Duration::from_millis(100)),  // Spinner animation
            Cmd::tick(Duration::from_secs(1)),      // Status refresh
        ])
    }
    
    fn update(&mut self, msg: HarnessMessage) -> Cmd<Self::Message> {
        match msg {
            HarnessMessage::LogLine(line) => {
                self.log_viewer.push(line);
                Cmd::none()
            }
            HarnessMessage::ToolStart(name) => {
                self.current_tool = Some(name);
                Cmd::none()
            }
            HarnessMessage::ToolEnd => {
                self.current_tool = None;
                Cmd::none()
            }
            HarnessMessage::Key(key) => {
                self.handle_key(key)
            }
            HarnessMessage::Tick => {
                self.spinner.tick();
                Cmd::none()
            }
            HarnessMessage::Quit => Cmd::quit(),
        }
    }
    
    fn view(&self, frame: &mut Frame) {
        let area = frame.buffer.area();
        
        // Status bar at top
        let status_area = Rect::new(0, 0, area.width, 1);
        self.status_bar.render(status_area, &mut frame.buffer);
        
        // Log viewer in middle
        let log_area = Rect::new(0, 1, area.width, area.height - self.ui_height);
        self.log_viewer.render(log_area, &mut frame.buffer);
        
        // Input line at bottom
        let input_area = Rect::new(0, area.height - 2, area.width, 2);
        self.input_line.render(input_area, &mut frame.buffer);
        
        // Spinner in corner
        let spinner_area = Rect::new(area.width - 2, area.height - 1, 2, 1);
        if self.current_tool.is_some() {
            self.spinner.render(spinner_area, &mut frame.buffer);
        }
    }
}
```

## Key Features to Demonstrate

### 1. Streaming Log Output
```rust
// Simulate tool output arriving in chunks
async fn stream_tool_output(tx: Sender<HarnessMessage>) {
    let output = run_tool().await;
    for line in output.lines() {
        tx.send(HarnessMessage::LogLine(line.to_string())).await?;
        // Log lines appear smoothly without flicker
    }
}
```

### 2. Inline Mode Stability
```rust
fn main() -> Result<()> {
    App::new(AgentHarness::new())
        .screen_mode(ScreenMode::Inline { ui_height: 4 })
        .run()
    // Native scrollback preserved
    // UI never corrupts scrollback
    // Cursor returns to correct position
}
```

### 3. Modal Alt-Screen (Optional)
```rust
impl AgentHarness {
    fn enter_file_picker(&mut self) -> Cmd<HarnessMessage> {
        // Temporarily switch to alt-screen for full-screen picker
        Cmd::batch(vec![
            Cmd::enter_alt_screen(),
            Cmd::msg(HarnessMessage::ShowPicker),
        ])
    }
    
    fn exit_file_picker(&mut self) -> Cmd<HarnessMessage> {
        Cmd::batch(vec![
            Cmd::exit_alt_screen(),
            Cmd::msg(HarnessMessage::PickerClosed),
        ])
    }
}
```

### 4. Panic Recovery
```rust
// Panic anywhere should restore terminal
std::panic::set_hook(Box::new(|info| {
    // Terminal cleanup happens via RAII guards
    eprintln!("Panic: {}", info);
}));
```

### 5. PTY Subprocess Capture (Feature-Gated)
```rust
#[cfg(feature = "pty")]
fn run_subprocess_with_capture() {
    let (master, slave) = openpty()?;
    let child = Command::new("cargo")
        .args(["test"])
        .stdin(slave)
        .stdout(slave)
        .stderr(slave)
        .spawn()?;
    
    // Read from master, forward to log viewer
    // All output goes through ftui (one-writer rule preserved)
}
```

 {"id":"bd-3emq","title":"Async Validation: Deterministic Concurrency Contract + Golden Trace","description":"## Goal
Make async validation behavior deterministic under a seeded scheduler and prove no stale results can override newer input.

## Core Idea (Extreme Optimization + Correctness)
- Monotonic validation token per input change; results must include token.
- Apply result only if token matches current input token.
- Record validation events in a trace (start/cancel/complete/apply).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Stale results are always ignored with explicit log entries.
- Deterministic trace under fixed seed and scripted input.
- Golden trace checksum matches after refactors (isomorphism proof).

## Tests (REQUIRED)
- unit_token_monotonic: tokens strictly increase per input.
- unit_stale_result_ignored: older token never applies.
- unit_trace_contains_all_events: no missing transitions.

## E2E (REQUIRED)
- Seeded async validator test harness with golden trace hash.
- Log: token, state, duration, decision per validation.

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures.
- Property tests: invariants (idempotence, monotonicity, determinism) where applicable.
- Fuzz tests: parsers/state machines and any input-facing logic.
- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.

 {"id":"bd-3fu8","title":"Semantic Events: Core Types and SemanticEvent Enum","description":"## Task

Define the core `SemanticEvent` enum and related types in `ftui-core`.

## Implementation

Create `crates/ftui-core/src/semantic_event.rs`:

```rust
use crate::{KeyCode, Modifiers, MouseButton, Position};
use std::time::Duration;

/// High-level semantic events derived from raw terminal input.
/// These represent user intentions rather than raw input.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq)]
pub enum SemanticEvent {
    // === Mouse Gestures ===
    
    /// Single click (mouse down + up in same position)
    Click {
        pos: Position,
        button: MouseButton,
    },
    
    /// Two clicks within threshold time
    DoubleClick {
        pos: Position,
        button: MouseButton,
    },
    
    /// Three clicks within threshold (often used for line selection)
    TripleClick {
        pos: Position,
        button: MouseButton,
    },
    
    /// Mouse held down beyond threshold without moving
    LongPress {
        pos: Position,
        duration: Duration,
    },
    
    // === Drag Gestures ===
    
    /// Mouse moved beyond drag threshold while button held
    DragStart {
        pos: Position,
        button: MouseButton,
    },
    
    /// Ongoing drag movement
    DragMove {
        start: Position,
        current: Position,
        delta: (i16, i16),  // Movement since last DragMove
    },
    
    /// Mouse released after drag
    DragEnd {
        start: Position,
        end: Position,
    },
    
    /// Drag cancelled (Escape pressed, focus lost, etc.)
    DragCancel,
    
    // === Keyboard Gestures ===
    
    /// Key chord sequence completed (e.g., Ctrl+K Ctrl+C)
    Chord {
        sequence: Vec<ChordKey>,
    },
    
    // === Touch-Like Gestures ===
    
    /// Swipe gesture (rapid mouse movement)
    Swipe {
        direction: SwipeDirection,
        distance: u16,
        velocity: f32,  // cells per second
    },
}

/// A key in a chord sequence
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
pub struct ChordKey {
    pub code: KeyCode,
    pub modifiers: Modifiers,
}

/// Direction for swipe gestures
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum SwipeDirection {
    Up,
    Down,
    Left,
    Right,
}

impl SemanticEvent {
    /// Returns true if this is a drag-related event
    pub fn is_drag(&self) -> bool {
        matches\!(self, Self::DragStart { .. } | Self::DragMove { .. } | Self::DragEnd { .. } | Self::DragCancel)
    }
    
    /// Returns the position if this event has one
    pub fn position(&self) -> Option<Position> {
        match self {
            Self::Click { pos, .. } |
            Self::DoubleClick { pos, .. } |
            Self::TripleClick { pos, .. } |
            Self::LongPress { pos, .. } |
            Self::DragStart { pos, .. } => Some(*pos),
            Self::DragMove { current, .. } => Some(*current),
            Self::DragEnd { end, .. } => Some(*end),
            _ => None,
        }
    }
}
```

## Unit Tests

```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[test]
    fn semantic_event_is_drag() {
        assert\!(SemanticEvent::DragStart { 
            pos: Position { x: 0, y: 0 }, 
            button: MouseButton::Left 
        }.is_drag());
        
        assert\!(\!SemanticEvent::Click { 
            pos: Position { x: 0, y: 0 }, 
            button: MouseButton::Left 
        }.is_drag());
    }

    #[test]
    fn semantic_event_position() {
        let click = SemanticEvent::Click { 
            pos: Position { x: 5, y: 10 }, 
            button: MouseButton::Left 
        };
        assert_eq\!(click.position(), Some(Position { x: 5, y: 10 }));
        
        let chord = SemanticEvent::Chord { sequence: vec\![] };
        assert_eq\!(chord.position(), None);
    }

    #[test]
    fn chord_key_equality() {
        let k1 = ChordKey { code: KeyCode::Char('k'), modifiers: Modifiers::CONTROL };
        let k2 = ChordKey { code: KeyCode::Char('k'), modifiers: Modifiers::CONTROL };
        let k3 = ChordKey { code: KeyCode::Char('c'), modifiers: Modifiers::CONTROL };
        
        assert_eq\!(k1, k2);
        assert_ne\!(k1, k3);
    }
}
```

pub fn enable_mouse_capture(writer: &mut impl Write) -> io::Result<()> {
    // Enable SGR extended mode (1006)

    write!(writer, "\x1b[?1000h\x1b[?1006h")?;

 {"id":"bd-3j8t","title":"Implement Virtualized<T> container with visible range calculation","description":"# Implement Virtualized<T> container with visible range calculation

## Background
Part of Progressive Rendering (bd-3t87). The core container that tracks scroll position and calculates which items are visible.

## Technical Design

```rust
/// Virtualized content container
pub struct Virtualized<T> {
    /// All items (owned or reference to external storage)
    items: VirtualizedStorage<T>,
    
    /// Current scroll offset (in items)
    scroll_offset: usize,
    
    /// Number of visible items (computed from area height)
    visible_count: usize,
    
    /// Overscan: extra items rendered above/below visible
    overscan: usize,
    
    /// Height per item
    item_height: ItemHeight,
    
    /// Whether to auto-scroll on new items
    follow_mode: bool,
    
    /// Scroll velocity for momentum
    scroll_velocity: f32,
}

/// Storage strategy for items
pub enum VirtualizedStorage<T> {
    /// Owned vector of items
    Owned(Vec<T>),
    
    /// External storage with fetch function
    External {
        len: usize,
        fetch: Box<dyn Fn(Range<usize>) -> Vec<T>>,
    },
}

/// Height calculation strategy
pub enum ItemHeight {
    /// All items have fixed height
    Fixed(u16),
    
    /// Items have variable height, cached
    Variable(HeightCache),
}

/// LRU cache for measured item heights
pub struct HeightCache {
    cache: LruCache<usize, u16>,
    default_height: u16,
}

impl<T> Virtualized<T> {
    /// Create with owned storage
    pub fn new(items: Vec<T>) -> Self {
        Self {
            items: VirtualizedStorage::Owned(items),
            scroll_offset: 0,
            visible_count: 0,
            overscan: 2,
            item_height: ItemHeight::Fixed(1),
            follow_mode: false,
            scroll_velocity: 0.0,
        }
    }
    
    /// Create with external storage
    pub fn external<F>(len: usize, fetch: F) -> Self
    where
        F: Fn(Range<usize>) -> Vec<T> + 'static,
    {
        Self {
            items: VirtualizedStorage::External { len, fetch: Box::new(fetch) },
            scroll_offset: 0,
            visible_count: 0,
            overscan: 2,
            item_height: ItemHeight::Fixed(1),
            follow_mode: false,
            scroll_velocity: 0.0,
        }
    }
    
    /// Total number of items
    pub fn len(&self) -> usize {
        match &self.items {
            VirtualizedStorage::Owned(v) => v.len(),
            VirtualizedStorage::External { len, .. } => *len,
        }
    }
    
    /// Calculate visible range given viewport height
    pub fn visible_range(&self, viewport_height: u16) -> Range<usize> {
        let items_per_screen = match &self.item_height {
            ItemHeight::Fixed(h) => (viewport_height / h) as usize,
            ItemHeight::Variable(cache) => {
                // Sum heights until we exceed viewport
                let mut count = 0;
                let mut total_height = 0u16;
                let start = self.scroll_offset;
                while total_height < viewport_height && start + count < self.len() {
                    total_height += cache.get(start + count);
                    count += 1;
                }
                count
            }
        };
        
        self.visible_count = items_per_screen;
        
        let start = self.scroll_offset;
        let end = (start + items_per_screen).min(self.len());
        start..end
    }
    
    /// Get range with overscan for smooth scrolling
    pub fn render_range(&self, viewport_height: u16) -> Range<usize> {
        let visible = self.visible_range(viewport_height);
        let start = visible.start.saturating_sub(self.overscan);
        let end = (visible.end + self.overscan).min(self.len());
        start..end
    }
    
    /// Get item by index
    pub fn get(&self, idx: usize) -> Option<&T> {
        match &self.items {
            VirtualizedStorage::Owned(v) => v.get(idx),
            VirtualizedStorage::External { .. } => None, // Must use fetch
        }
    }
    
    /// Update external storage length
    pub fn set_len(&mut self, len: usize) {
        if let VirtualizedStorage::External { len: ref mut l, .. } = &mut self.items {
            *l = len;
        }
    }
}

impl HeightCache {
    pub fn new(default_height: u16, capacity: usize) -> Self {
        Self {
            cache: LruCache::new(NonZeroUsize::new(capacity).unwrap()),
            default_height,
        }
    }
    
    pub fn get(&self, idx: usize) -> u16 {
        *self.cache.get(&idx).unwrap_or(&self.default_height)
    }
    
    pub fn set(&mut self, idx: usize, height: u16) {
        self.cache.put(idx, height);
    }
}
```
